title: Mahabat Khan Mosque

infobox.value: [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam")
Muhammad Tayyab Qureshi (Khateeb)
[Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")
[34°00′38″N 71°34′23″E﻿ / ﻿34.01065°N 71.57318°E﻿ / 34.01065; 71.57318](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Mahabat_Khan_Mosque&params=34.01065_N_71.57318_E_region:PK_type:landmark)
Mosque
2 functional, 8 decorative
* [Amburiq](/wiki/Ambur

([Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu_language "Urdu language"): مہابت خان مسجد; [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language"): مهابت خان جومات), sometimes spelt **Mohabbat Khan Mosque**, is a 17th-century [Mughal](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire")-era mosque in [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). The mosque was built in 1630, and named after the Mughal governor of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), Mahabat Khān Kamboh.[[a]](#cite_note-8) The mosque's [white marble](/wiki/Marble "Marble") façade is considered to be one of Peshawar's most iconic sights.

Speculated to be of varying identification and origin.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) Written to be [Ali Mardan Khan](/wiki/Ali_Mardan_Khan "Ali Mardan Khan")[[3]](#cite_note-3) or the father of [Khairandesh Khan](/wiki/Nawab_Khair_Andesh_Khan "Nawab Khair Andesh Khan").[[4]](#cite_note-4) As well as *Zaman Khan* under the alias Mahabat Khan.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Also written to match the name of Zamána Beg, son of Ghór Beg, a native of [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)
Shah, Ibrahim *[The Mahabat Khan Mosque: Historical Perspective](https://www.proquest.com/docview/1301942533?sourcetype=Scholarly%20Journals)* Pakistan Historical Society
*[Rewind: Untying the myth of Mahabat, Qasim, Gunj and Dilawar](https://tribune.com.pk/story/771928/rewind-untying-the-myth-of-mahabat-qasim-gunj-and-dilawar)* (2014) Tribune

Directorate of Archaeology & Museums *[Mahabat Khan](https://kparchaeology.gkp.pk/mahabat_khan.php#:~:text=The%20Mahabat%20Khan%20Mosque%2C%20sometimes,Khan%20and%20Ali%20Mardan%20Khan.)* Govt. of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Peshawar
Vadivelu, A. (1915). *The Ruling Chiefs, Nobles and Zamindars of India, Volume 1*. G.C. Loganadham. p. 534.
NWFP dpt. *["Yearbook"](https://www.google.com/books/edition/Yearbook/rqQ0AQAAIAAJ?kptab=editions&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjJg4uTnO2LAxVk2zQHHVcqKF84KBCYFnoECAwQCA)* (1950)

*[From Landikotal to Wagah](https://www.google.com.au/books/edition/From_Landi_Kotal_to_Wagah/AR4MEAAAQBAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=mahabat+khan+mosque&pg=PA40&printsec=frontcover)* by Salman Rashid (2020) Sang-e-Meel Publications

*[The Oriental Biographical Dictionary](https://www.google.com.au/books/edition/The_Oriental_Biographical_Dictionary/JZctk5Kbf2wC?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=nawab+mahabat+khan+mosque&pg=PA161&printsec=frontcover)* by Beal, 1881

["Mahabat Khan Mosque"](https://books.google.com/books?id=PK_XAAAAMAAJ&q=khan+mosque+peshawar). *Pharos:Research Journal of the Shaykh Zayed Islamic Centre*. **3** (11). University of Peshawar. 1996. Retrieved 15 September 2017.

Nadiem, Ihsan (2007). [*Peshawar: heritage, history, monuments*](https://books.google.com/books?id=9_1tAAAAMAAJ&q=mohabbat+khan+mosque+peshawar). Sang-e-Meel Publications. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9789693519716](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9789693519716 "Special:BookSources/9789693519716"). Retrieved 15 September 2017.
[*Gazetteer of the Dera Ghazi Khan District: 1883*](https://books.google.com/books?id=LpwIAAAAQAAJ&dq=mohabbat+khan+mosque+peshawar&pg=RA3-PT31). 1883. Retrieved 15 September 2017.

Kakar, Mohammed (1997). [*Afghanistan: The Soviet Invasion and the Afghan Response, 1979–1982*](https://books.google.com/books?id=X68wDwAAQBAJ&dq=khan+mosque+peshawar&pg=PA97). University of California Press. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780520208933](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780520208933 "Special:BookSources/9780520208933"). Retrieved 15 September 2017.

Khan, Ahmad Nabi (1991). [*Development of Mosque Architecture in Pakistan*](https://books.google.com/books?id=yvLVAAAAMAAJ&q=khan+mosque+peshawar). Lok Virsa Publishing House. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9789694680088](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9789694680088 "Special:BookSources/9789694680088"). Retrieved 15 September 2017.
[*The Journal of Humanities and Social Sciences, Volume 12*](https://books.google.com/books?id=HSUUAQAAMAAJ&q=khan+mosque+peshawar). University of Peshawar. 2004. Retrieved 15 September 2017.

[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system"): [34°00′38″N 71°34′23″E﻿ / ﻿34.01065°N 71.57318°E﻿ / 34.01065; 71.57318](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Mahabat_Khan_Mosque&params=34.01065_N_71.57318_E_region:PK_type:landmark)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Mughal-era mosque in Peshawar, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan

| Mahabat Khan Mosque | |
| --- | --- |
| مہابت خان مسجد | |
| The white marble façade of the mosque is one of the most iconic sights in Peshawar | |
| Religion | |
| [Affiliation](/wiki/List_of_religions_and_spiritual_traditions "List of religions and spiritual traditions") | [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") |
| Leadership | Muhammad Tayyab Qureshi (Khateeb) |
| Location | |
| Location | [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") |
| [Map](/wiki/Special:Map/13/34.01065/71.57318/en) Interactive map of Mahabat Khan Mosque | |
| Coordinates | [34°00′38″N 71°34′23″E﻿ / ﻿34.01065°N 71.57318°E﻿ / 34.01065; 71.57318](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Mahabat_Khan_Mosque&params=34.01065_N_71.57318_E_region:PK_type:landmark) |
| Architecture | |
| Type | Mosque |
| Completed | 1670 |
| [Minarets](/wiki/Minaret "Minaret") | 2 functional, 8 decorative |

The **Mahabat Khan Mosque** ([Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu_language "Urdu language"): مہابت خان مسجد; [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language"): مهابت خان جومات), sometimes spelt **Mohabbat Khan Mosque**, is a 17th-century [Mughal](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire")-era mosque in [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). The mosque was built in 1630, and named after the Mughal governor of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), Mahabat Khān Kamboh.[[a]](#cite_note-8) The mosque's [white marble](/wiki/Marble "Marble") façade is considered to be one of Peshawar's most iconic sights.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mahabat_Khan_Mosque&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The mosque was built between 1660 and 1670 by the [Mughals](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"),[[8]](#cite_note-9) on what was the highest point in the old city.[[9]](#cite_note-dawn-10)

The minarets of the Mohabbat Khan Mosque were frequently used in [Sikh times](/wiki/Sikh_Empire "Sikh Empire") for hanging prisoners. Five people per day were hanged from the minarets because they banned 5 times Azan for prayer,[[10]](#cite_note-11) as a substitute for the gallows.[[11]](#cite_note-12) Following the [Soviet invasion of Afghanistan](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War"), refugee tribal elders would congregate in the mosque in order to forge unity amongst Afghans against the Soviets.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

## Layout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mahabat_Khan_Mosque&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Layout")]

The mosque is 30,155 square feet in size.[[9]](#cite_note-dawn-10) Its open courtyard has a centrally located ablution pool and a single row of rooms lining the exterior walls.

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mahabat_Khan_Mosque&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Architecture")]

The prayer hall occupies the west side. The hall is flanked by two tall minarets, which are divided into three sections.[[9]](#cite_note-dawn-10) The façade of the prayer hall is also capped by 6 smaller decorative minarets that flank the mosque's 5 arched entryways, with an additional 2 minarets flanking the set of 6. The prayer hall is capped by 3 fluted domes. The roofline rises from the outer edges, towards the centre by a series of four small incremental height increases. The roofline is embellished with numerous [merlons](/wiki/Merlon "Merlon").[[13]](#cite_note-14) The top of the mosque's white marble façade is capped by [cavettos](/wiki/Cavetto "Cavetto"), or concave moulding.[[9]](#cite_note-dawn-10)

5 arched portals offer entry into the main prayer hall. The central arch is the tallest, and features [cusped arches](/wiki/Multifoil_arch "Multifoil arch") typical of the Mughal style. The central arch is flanked by two slightly shorter un-cusped arches, that are designed in the Persian and Central Asian style. These arches are flanked by a smaller arch decorated in a similar style, and row of 7 small arched portals are found above the tip of each arch. The three central arched portals are embellished with [muqarnas](/wiki/Muqarna "Muqarna") above the row of 7 mini-arched portals, while the outermost arches are instead decorated with *ghalib kari*, or a network of ribs made of stucco and plaster that are applied to curved surfaces in the archways for decorative purposes. Archways into the mosque are also flanked by vegetal motifs along their upper curves, which unlike the green motifs at [Badshahi Mosque](/wiki/Badshahi_Mosque "Badshahi Mosque"), are multi-coloured.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

Both the interior and exterior feature panels are embellished with floral motifs and Quranic calligraphy.[[9]](#cite_note-dawn-10) The interior of the prayer hall is sheltered beneath the three low fluted domes and is eloquently painted with floral and geometric designs.[[9]](#cite_note-dawn-10)

## Gallery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mahabat_Khan_Mosque&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Gallery")]

* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/MohabbatKhanMosque.png/120px-MohabbatKhanMosque.png)](/wiki/File:MohabbatKhanMosque.png)
* [![The minarets of the mosque feature cupolas and overhanging eaves](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/PeshawarMahabatKhanM.jpg/120px-PeshawarMahabatKhanM.jpg)](/wiki/File:PeshawarMahabatKhanM.jpg "The minarets of the mosque feature cupolas and overhanging eaves")

  The minarets of the mosque feature [cupolas](/wiki/Cupola "Cupola") and overhanging [eaves](/wiki/Eaves "Eaves")
* [![Peshawar's Jeweler's Bazaar is located next to the mosque.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Flickr_-_boellstiftung_-_Altstadt_Peshawar.jpg/120px-Flickr_-_boellstiftung_-_Altstadt_Peshawar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_boellstiftung_-_Altstadt_Peshawar.jpg "Peshawar's Jeweler's Bazaar is located next to the mosque.")

  Peshawar's Jeweler's Bazaar is located next to the mosque.
* [![A minaret of the mosque](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Masjid_mahabat_khan_bin_ali.jpg/120px-Masjid_mahabat_khan_bin_ali.jpg)](/wiki/File:Masjid_mahabat_khan_bin_ali.jpg "A minaret of the mosque")

  A minaret of the mosque
